items.q: What are the four Ps of the marketing mix?
items.correct: Product, Price, Place (distribution), and Promotion — the controllable variables a business uses to satisfy customers and achieve objectives.
items.misconception: The four Ps are Product, Price, Profit, and People.
items.random_wrong: The four Ps are Planning, Producing, Promoting, and Profit.
items.why: Students often substitute 'profit' or 'people' into the four Ps. The classic McCarthy framework is Product, Price, Place and Promotion — extended models add others but those four are foundational.

items.q: What is a brand in marketing?
items.correct: A combination of name, design, symbols and associations that distinguishes a product and creates customer perception and loyalty — more than just a logo.
items.misconception: A brand is just the logo on a product.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the company that makes a product.
items.why: Branding is the sum of perceptions, not just visual identity. Coca-Cola the brand is much more than the script logo — it is associations, taste expectations, advertising history.

items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.

items.q: A new innovative tech gadget launching at a HIGH price is using.
items.why: Correct answer: Skimming pricing. “Penetration pricing” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Psychological. “Cost-plus” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Cost per unit £40. Mark-up 25%. What is the selling price, in £?
items.why: Exact answer: 50. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
